acute inattention with fever and urinary infection signs

exhibits.title: Timeline
items: Today: Pulling at lines and febrile

questions.stem: Which finding is the most important cue for the nurse to recognize first?

options.text: Fall precautions
acute inattention with fever and urinary infection signs
HR: 116/min

questions.rationale: acute inattention with fever and urinary infection signs is the cue that makes this case unsafe because it points to delirium likely triggered by infection and dehydration. Recognizing it first keeps the nurse focused on the immediate threat instead of routine data.

distractorRationales.a: "Fall precautions" may be relevant, but it does not signal the immediate delirium likely triggered by infection and dehydration risk as strongly as acute inattention with fever and urinary infection signs.
distractorRationales.c: "HR: 116/min" may be relevant, but it does not signal the immediate delirium likely triggered by infection and dehydration risk as strongly as acute inattention with fever and urinary infection signs.
distractorRationales.d: "WBC: 15,600/mm3" may be relevant, but it does not signal the immediate delirium likely triggered by infection and dehydration risk as strongly as acute inattention with fever and urinary infection signs.

questions.takeaway: Recognize acute inattention with fever and urinary infection signs as the cue that changes the pace of care.

questions.scenario: A 38-year-old client in the urgent transfer report has high-risk findings that require immediate escalation. The client has fever, suspected infection, cool extremities, and a falling blood pressure despite increasing tachycardia.

exhibits.title: Nurses' Note
exhibits.body: The client has fever, suspected infection, cool extremities, and a falling blood pressure despite increasing tachycardia.

exhibits.title: Focused Assessment
items: high-risk findings that require immediate escalation

leftActions.text: Obtain cultures and start broad-spectrum antibiotics
Administer isotonic crystalloid bolus as prescribed
Encourage oral fluids and reassess in 2 hours
Give acetaminophen as the primary intervention

rightMonitoring.text: Lactate trend and mean arterial pressure

questions.rationale: The pattern is septic shock with impaired perfusion. Early antibiotics, cultures, fluids, lactate, MAP, and urine output address the time-sensitive threats.

distractorRationales.sepsis-oral-fluids: Encourage oral fluids and reassess in 2 hours does not address the immediate sepsis / perfusion priority and could delay stabilization.
distractorRationales.sepsis-antipyretic-only: Give acetaminophen as the primary intervention does not address the immediate sepsis / perfusion priority and could delay stabilization.
distractorRationales.sepsis-daily-weight: Daily weight after stabilization does not address the immediate sepsis / perfusion priority and could delay stabilization.
distractorRationales.sepsis-skin-turgor: Skin turgor alone does not address the immediate sepsis / perfusion priority and could delay stabilization.

structuredRationale.overview: The safest answer is Center: sepsis-condition; LeftActions: sepsis-cultures-antibiotics, sepsis-crystalloid-bolus; RightMonitoring: sepsis-lactate-map, sepsis-urine-output. questions.stem: Complete the bow-tie for the client in the step-down unit.
questions.scenario: A 52-year-old client in the step-down unit has rapid deterioration after two hours of observation. The postoperative client develops sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, and anxiety.

leftActions.text: Apply oxygen and position with head of bed elevated

questions.stem: Complete the bow-tie for the client in the urgent transfer report.
questions.scenario: A 38-year-old client in the urgent transfer report has high-risk findings that require immediate escalation. The pregnant client has severe headache, visual changes, hyperreflexia, and severe-range blood pressure.

exhibits.title: Nurses' Note
exhibits.body: The pregnant client has severe headache, visual changes, hyperreflexia, and severe-range blood pressure.

exhibits.title: Focused Assessment
items: high-risk findings that require immediate escalation
Eclampsia or severe preeclampsia with seizure risk

center.text: Eclampsia or severe preeclampsia with seizure risk

leftActions.text: Administer magnesium sulfate as prescribed
Maintain seizure precautions and left-lateral positioning
Place in a dark room as the only intervention
Encourage walking to reduce anxiety

rightMonitoring.text: Respirations, deep tendon reflexes, and level of consciousness

questions.rationale: Magnesium reduces seizure risk, but toxicity can suppress respirations and reflexes. Blood pressure and urine output reflect maternal perfusion and renal involvement.

distractorRationales.eclampsia-dark-room-only: Place in a dark room as the only intervention does not address the immediate ob / hypertensive emergency priority and could delay stabilization.
distractorRationales.eclampsia-encourage-walking: Encourage walking to reduce anxiety does not address the immediate ob / hypertensive emergency priority and could delay stabilization.
distractorRationales.eclampsia-fetal-kicks-only: Kick counts only does not address the immediate ob / hypertensive emergency priority and could delay stabilization.
distractorRationales.eclampsia-appetite: Appetite and meal completion does not address the immediate ob / hypertensive emergency priority and could delay stabilization.

conceptNotes: Eclampsia or severe preeclampsia with seizure risk

questions.stem: Complete the bow-tie for the client in the step-down unit.
questions.scenario: A 52-year-old client in the step-down unit has rapid deterioration after two hours of observation. The client develops throat tightness, wheezing, urticaria, and hypotension shortly after a new medication.

exhibits.title: Nurses' Note
exhibits.body: The client develops throat tightness, wheezing, urticaria, and hypotension shortly after a new medication.

exhibits.title: Diagnostic Data
items: Medication administration record shows new antibiotic

exhibits.title: Focused Assessment
items: rapid deterioration after two hours of observation
Anaphylaxis with airway and perfusion threat

center.text: Anaphylaxis with airway and perfusion threat

leftActions.text: Administer intramuscular epinephrine immediately
Support airway and give high-flow oxygen
Give oral antihistamine and wait for effect
Finish the medication infusion slowly

rightMonitoring.text: Airway swelling, breath sounds, and oxygenation

questions.rationale: Anaphylaxis is an immediate airway and circulatory emergency. Epinephrine and airway support are first-line while monitoring for recurrent bronchospasm or hypotension.

distractorRationales.anaphylaxis-oral-antihistamine: Give oral antihistamine and wait for effect does not address the immediate immune / airway priority and could delay stabilization.
distractorRationales.anaphylaxis-finish-infusion: Finish the medication infusion slowly does not address the immediate immune / airway priority and could delay stabilization.
distractorRationales.anaphylaxis-rash-only: Rash color only does not address the immediate immune / airway priority and could delay stabilization.
distractorRationales.anaphylaxis-temperature: Temperature every 4 hours does not address the immediate immune / airway priority and could delay stabilization.

conceptNotes: Anaphylaxis with airway and perfusion threat

structuredRationale.overview: The safest answer is Center: anaphylaxis-condition; LeftActions: anaphylaxis-epinephrine, anaphylaxis-airway-oxygen; RightMonitoring: anaphylaxis-airway-breathing, anaphylaxis-bp-rebound. The clinical focus is adrenal crisis with shock risk, so the review should connect the answer to adrenal, cortisol, steroid, shock.

questions.stem: Complete the bow-tie for the client in the emergency department.

items: new confusion and worsening vital-sign instability
Eclampsia or severe preeclampsia with seizure risk

center.text: Eclampsia or severe preeclampsia with seizure risk

leftActions.text: Administer magnesium sulfate as prescribed
Maintain seizure precautions and left-lateral positioning
Place in a dark room as the only intervention
Encourage walking to reduce anxiety

rightMonitoring.text: Respirations, deep tendon reflexes, and level of consciousness

questions.rationale: Magnesium reduces seizure risk, but toxicity can suppress respirations and reflexes. Blood pressure and urine output reflect maternal perfusion and renal involvement.

distractorRationales.eclampsia-appetite: Appetite and meal completion does not address the immediate ob / hypertensive emergency priority and could delay stabilization.

items: ECG shows ST elevation in contiguous leads

items: new confusion and worsening vital-sign instability
Acute coronary syndrome with evolving infarction

center.text: Acute coronary syndrome with evolving infarction

leftActions.text: Administer chewable aspirin if not contraindicated
Activate reperfusion pathway and notify the provider
Ambulate to assess exertional tolerance
Delay ECG review until pain medication works

rightMonitoring.text: Chest pain, blood pressure, and perfusion

questions.rationale: STEMI care requires rapid antiplatelet therapy and reperfusion coordination. Rhythm, ST changes, pain, and perfusion show whether ischemia is progressing.

questions.stem: Complete the bow-tie for the client in the step-down unit.
questions.scenario: A 52-year-old client in the step-down unit has rapid deterioration after two hours of observation. The client is difficult to arouse with pinpoint pupils and slow shallow respirations after receiving opioid medication.

exhibits.title: Nurses' Note
exhibits.body: The client is difficult to arouse with pinpoint pupils and slow shallow respirations after receiving opioid medication.

exhibits.title: Diagnostic Data
items: Medication record shows recent opioid dose

exhibits.title: Focused Assessment
items: rapid deterioration after two hours of observation

leftActions.text: Support airway and ventilation with oxygen
Administer naloxone as prescribed
Allow the client to sleep and reassess later
Give another opioid dose for comfort

rightMonitoring.text: Sedation level and recurrent respiratory depression

questions.rationale: Ventilation comes first because opioid toxicity suppresses respiratory drive. Naloxone can wear off before the opioid, so recurrent sedation and oxygenation require close monitoring.

distractorRationales.opioid-overdose-let-sleep: Allow the client to sleep and reassess later does not address the immediate toxicology / respiratory depression priority and could delay stabilization.

distractorRationales.opioid-overdose-appetite: Appetite after awakening does not address the immediate toxicology / respiratory depression priority and could delay stabilization.
distractorRationales.opioid-overdose-skin-temp: Skin temperature only does not address the immediate toxicology / respiratory depression priority and could delay stabilization.

questions.stem: For each postoperative task, click to specify whether the task should stay with the RN or can be delegated to the UAP.
questions.scenario: The RN is caring for several postoperative clients and is working with an experienced UAP.
questions.additionalInfo: The RN must decide which work can safely move and which work must stay with licensed nursing judgment.

options.text: Keep with RN
Delegate to UAP

matrixRows.label: Assess a client with new shortness of breath two hours after surgery
Assist a stable client to the bathroom for the first time after the RN has assessed
Reinforce coughing and deep-breathing after the RN provides the initial teaching
Evaluate a new report of uncontrolled incisional pain

questions.rationale: Delegation becomes faster when the nurse separates stable, predictable tasks from new assessment or evaluation needs. Acute respiratory change and uncontrolled pain require RN judgment, while routine assistance and reinforcement after assessment can move safely to the UAP.

distractorRationales.a: "Keep with RN" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Acute respiratory change and uncontrolled pain require RN judgment" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.b: The cue "Delegation becomes faster when the nurse separates stable, predictable tasks from new assessment or evaluation needs" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "Delegate to UAP" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: New problems stay with the RN; stable, routine assistance can move.
questions.speedCue: Ask whether the task needs fresh assessment or evaluation. If yes, it stays with the RN.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each finding, click to specify whether the finding supports worsening acute kidney injury or a less urgent monitoring picture.
questions.scenario: The nurse is comparing several renal function trends for a client whose kidney status is worsening.
questions.additionalInfo: The provider asks the nurse to identify which findings point to deterioration that needs faster action.

options.text: Supports worsening acute kidney injury
Less urgent monitoring picture

matrixRows.label: Rising creatinine with falling urine output
Progressive edema and increasing potassium
Stable creatinine with adequate hourly urine output
Improved intake with steady daily weight

questions.rationale: Acute kidney injury becomes more urgent when urine output falls, creatinine rises, and electrolyte complications emerge. Stable output and renal trends belong on the less urgent side because they do not signal deterioration.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Supports worsening acute kidney injury" moves care toward routine care before renal protection.
distractorRationales.b: The option can leave renal injury untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "the row-specific answer pattern" fits the safer response to the cue "Falling urine plus rising creatinine is the shortcut to worsening AKI".

questions.takeaway: Falling urine plus rising creatinine is the shortcut to worsening AKI.
questions.speedCue: Creatinine up and urine down means kidney injury is moving the wrong way.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each maternal finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with placental abruption or less consistent with placental abruption.
questions.scenario: The client is 35 weeks pregnant and arrives after reporting severe abdominal pain.

options.text: More consistent with placental abruption
Less consistent with placental abruption

matrixRows.label: Painful dark bleeding
Rigid tender uterus
Painless bright-red bleeding
Bleeding only after vaginal exam

questions.rationale: Placental abruption becomes easier to answer when the learner locks onto pain, uterine tone, and fetal compromise clues. Painless bright-red bleeding points away from abruption and more toward placenta previa.

distractorRationales.a: "More consistent with placental abruption" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Abruption is the painful, rigid, often dark-bleeding pattern" points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "The client is 35 weeks pregnant and arrives after reporting severe abdominal pain" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.

questions.stem: For each finding, click to specify whether the finding supports worsening acute kidney injury or a less urgent monitoring picture.
questions.scenario: The nurse is comparing several renal function trends for a client whose kidney status is worsening.
questions.additionalInfo: The provider asks the nurse to identify which findings point to deterioration that needs faster action.

options.text: Supports worsening acute kidney injury
Less urgent monitoring picture

matrixRows.label: Rising creatinine with falling urine output
Progressive edema and increasing potassium
Stable creatinine with adequate hourly urine output
Improved intake with steady daily weight

questions.rationale: Acute kidney injury becomes more urgent when urine output falls, creatinine rises, and electrolyte complications emerge. Stable output and renal trends belong on the less urgent side because they do not signal deterioration.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of renal protection. The stem cue "Falling urine plus rising creatinine is the shortcut to worsening AKI" makes renal injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: The option can leave renal injury untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "the row-specific answer pattern" fits the safer response to the cue "Falling urine plus rising creatinine is the shortcut to worsening AKI".

questions.takeaway: Falling urine plus rising creatinine is the shortcut to worsening AKI.
questions.speedCue: Creatinine up and urine down means kidney injury is moving the wrong way.

questions.stem: For each client finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with neuroleptic malignant syndrome or less consistent with neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
questions.scenario: The client recently started a high-potency antipsychotic and now has fever and confusion.

options.text: More consistent with NMS
Less consistent with NMS

matrixRows.label: Lead-pipe rigidity
Autonomic instability
Marked hyperreflexia and diarrhea
Recent serotonergic antibiotic overlap

questions.rationale: NMS is easier to recognize when the learner locks onto severe rigidity and autonomic instability after dopamine-blocking medication exposure. Hyperreflexia and diarrhea push the pattern away from NMS and more toward serotonin syndrome.

distractorRationales.a: "More consistent with NMS" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Rigidity after antipsychotic exposure should move NMS higher than serotonin syndrome" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "NMS is easier to recognize when the learner locks onto severe rigidity and autonomic instability after dopamine-blocking medication" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Rigidity after antipsychotic exposure should move NMS higher than serotonin syndrome.
questions.speedCue: When a high-fever medication question includes rigidity, slow down and separate rigidity from hyperreflexia.

questions.stem: For each maternal finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with placental abruption or less consistent with placental abruption.
questions.scenario: The client is 35 weeks pregnant and arrives after reporting severe abdominal pain.

options.text: More consistent with placental abruption
Less consistent with placental abruption

matrixRows.label: Painful dark bleeding
Rigid tender uterus
Painless bright-red bleeding
Bleeding only after vaginal exam

questions.rationale: Placental abruption becomes easier to answer when the learner locks onto pain, uterine tone, and fetal compromise clues. Painless bright-red bleeding points away from abruption and more toward placenta previa.

distractorRationales.a: "More consistent with placental abruption" misses the bedside risk. distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Less consistent with placental abruption" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bleeding-risk intervention.

questions.takeaway: Abruption is the painful, rigid, often dark-bleeding pattern.
questions.speedCue: Pain and uterine rigidity should move abruption to the top of the differential before you even finish the stem.

questions.stem: For each finding, click to specify whether the finding supports worsening acute kidney injury or a less urgent monitoring picture.
questions.scenario: The nurse is comparing several renal function trends for a client whose kidney status is worsening.
questions.additionalInfo: The provider asks the nurse to identify which findings point to deterioration that needs faster action.

options.text: Supports worsening acute kidney injury
Less urgent monitoring picture

matrixRows.label: Rising creatinine with falling urine output
Progressive edema and increasing potassium
Stable creatinine with adequate hourly urine output
Improved intake with steady daily weight

questions.rationale: Acute kidney injury becomes more urgent when urine output falls, creatinine rises, and electrolyte complications emerge. Stable output and renal trends belong on the less urgent side because they do not signal deterioration.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of renal protection. The stem cue "For each finding, click to specify whether the finding supports worsening acute kidney injury or a less urgent" makes renal injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Less urgent monitoring picture" moves care toward routine care before renal protection.

questions.takeaway: Falling urine plus rising creatinine is the shortcut to worsening AKI.
questions.speedCue: Creatinine up and urine down means kidney injury is moving the wrong way.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

structuredRationale.overview: The safest answer is Rising Creatinine With Falling Urine Output: Supports worsening acute kidney injury; Progressive Edema And Increasing Potassium: Supports worsening acute kidney injury; Stable Creatinine With Adequate Hourly Urine Output: Less urgent monitoring picture; Improved Intake With Steady Daily Weight: Less urgent monitoring picture. Acute kidney injury becomes more urgent when urine output falls, creatinine rises, and electrolyte complications emerge. Stable output and renal trends belong on the less urgent side because they do not signal deterioration. The clinical focus is lithium toxicity and renal clearance, so the review should connect the answer to lithium, toxicity, renal, fluid.
structuredRationale.mechanism: This item tests Acute Kidney Injury RIFLE Staging through lithium toxicity and renal clearance: dehydration or reduced renal clearance raises lithium levels, increasing neurologic toxicity and safety risk. These lithium, toxicity, renal, fluid cues determine whether the nurse must act now, escalate care, or withhold routine teaching until safety is protected.

questions.stem: A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse sees a loop of umbilical cord. Which immediate actions should the nurse take?
questions.scenario: The fetal heart rate remains in the 70s while help is being called.

options.text: Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord
Position the client to relieve pressure on the cord
Attempt to push the visible cord back into the vagina
Delay intervention until the provider is at the bedside
Call for immediate obstetric assistance
Start coached pushing with the next contraction

questions.rationale: Cord prolapse is a compression emergency. The nurse's first moves are to relieve the pressure, position the client to reduce compression, and summon immediate obstetric help. Pushing the cord back or delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk.

distractorRationales.c: The cue "Cord prolapse is a compression emergency" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Attempt to push the visible cord back into the vagina" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: "Delay intervention until the provider is at the bedside" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Cord prolapse is a compression emergency" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.f: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Cord prolapse means relieve compression now, not when the provider arrives.
questions.speedCue: If you can see the cord and the heart rate crashes, think pressure-off-the-cord immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

structuredRationale.overview: The safest answer is A: Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord; B: Position the client to relieve pressure on the cord; E: Call for immediate obstetric assistance. Cord prolapse is a compression emergency. The nurse's first moves are to relieve the pressure, position the client to reduce compression, and summon immediate obstetric help. Pushing the cord back or delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk. The clinical focus is umbilical cord compression, so the review should connect the answer to cord, compression, fetal, bradycardia.
structuredRationale.mechanism: This item tests Cord Prolapse Positioning through umbilical cord compression: cord compression reduces fetal perfusion and oxygenation, so relieving pressure and mobilizing emergency birth support come first. These cord, compression, fetal, bradycardia cues determine whether the nurse must act now, escalate care, or withhold routine teaching until safety is protected.
structuredRationale.whyCorrect: A: Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord; B: Position the client to relieve pressure on the cord; E: Call for immediate obstetric assistance is correct because it directly addresses umbilical cord compression. cord compression reduces fetal perfusion and oxygenation, so relieving pressure and mobilizing emergency birth support come first. The attached study resources can reinforce NCLEX exam blueprint and NGN item format.

whyWrong.c: The cue "Cord prolapse is a compression emergency" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Attempt to push the visible cord back into the vagina" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up. Clinically, this misses umbilical cord compression: cord compression reduces fetal perfusion and oxygenation, so relieving pressure and mobilizing emergency birth support come first.
whyWrong.d: "Delay intervention until the provider is at the bedside" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Cord prolapse is a compression emergency" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine observation. Clinically, this misses umbilical cord compression: cord compression reduces fetal perfusion and oxygenation, so relieving pressure and mobilizing emergency birth support come first.
whyWrong.f: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern. Clinically, this misses umbilical cord compression: cord compression reduces fetal perfusion and oxygenation, so relieving pressure and mobilizing emergency birth support come first.

questions.stem: A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse sees a loop of umbilical cord. Which immediate actions should the nurse take?
questions.scenario: The fetal heart rate remains in the 70s while help is being called.

options.text: Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord
Position the client to relieve pressure on the cord
Attempt to push the visible cord back into the vagina
Delay intervention until the provider is at the bedside
Call for immediate obstetric assistance
Start coached pushing with the next contraction

questions.rationale: Cord prolapse is a compression emergency. The nurse's first moves are to relieve the pressure, position the client to reduce compression, and summon immediate obstetric help. Pushing the cord back or delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk.

distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Attempt to push the visible cord back into the vagina" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord plus Position the client" fits the safer response to the cue "A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse".
distractorRationales.f: "Start coached pushing with the next contraction" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Cord prolapse is a compression emergency" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before diagnostic reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Cord prolapse means relieve compression now, not when the provider arrives.
questions.speedCue: If you can see the cord and the heart rate crashes, think pressure-off-the-cord immediately.

questions.stem: Which findings support the nurse's concern that this newborn is becoming hypoglycemic? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A newborn of a gestational-diabetic parent is 3 hours old and is becoming harder to feed.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding whether the bedside change fits a glucose problem or a routine transition period.

options.text: Jitteriness with tremulous extremities
Weak or poor feeding effort
Low axillary temperature
Strong lusty cry with vigorous rooting
Pink color and normal spontaneous wakefulness

questions.rationale: Newborn hypoglycemia often shows up as jitteriness, poor feeding, and temperature instability. The nurse should cluster the subtle findings together quickly instead of waiting for a dramatic crash.

distractorRationales.d: "Strong lusty cry with vigorous rooting" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Newborn hypoglycemia often shows up as jitteriness, poor feeding" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.e: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "The nurse is deciding whether the bedside change fits a glucose problem or a routine transition period" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: In newborns, multiple small instability clues often matter more than one dramatic sign.
questions.speedCue: Jitteriness plus poor feeding should move glucose near the top immediately.

questions.stem: Which findings make severe symptomatic hyponatremia the best interpretation? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A client taking a thiazide diuretic becomes progressively more confused during admission.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding whether the change is mostly electrolyte collapse or routine fatigue.

options.text: New confusion with headache
Seizure activity after worsening mentation
Serum sodium of 118 mEq/L
Dry hacking cough for 1 week
Normal orientation and steady gait

questions.rationale: The key cluster is a very low sodium level plus acute neurologic deterioration. The nurse should read the lab and the brain symptoms together, because that combination changes the urgency immediately.

distractorRationales.d: The cue "The nurse is deciding whether the change is mostly electrolyte collapse or routine fatigue" suggests neurologic deterioration, so "Dry hacking cough for 1 week" would not correct the active problem. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.e: "Normal orientation and steady gait" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The key cluster is a very low sodium level plus acute neurologic deterioration" points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.

questions.takeaway: A sodium number matters more when the nervous system is already failing in front of you.
questions.speedCue: Low sodium plus neuro change is the shortcut; don't split the clue cluster apart.

questions.stem: A client with a positive pregnancy test reports sudden unilateral pelvic pain, dizziness, and left shoulder pain. Which findings support the nurse's concern for a ruptured ectopic pregnancy?
questions.scenario: The client looks pale and anxious in triage. Blood pressure is 86/48 mm Hg and heart rate is 128/min.

options.text: Hypotension
Shoulder pain
Rigid abdomen
Light pink spotting only
Tachycardia
Improving pelvic discomfort after rest

questions.rationale: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy should be recognized quickly from the clue cluster of unilateral pain, hypotension, tachycardia, and referred shoulder pain from intraperitoneal bleeding. The nurse should connect these findings to hemorrhagic instability instead of treating them as routine first-trimester discomfort.

distractorRationales.c: "Rigid abdomen" misses the bedside risk. The cue "A client with a positive pregnancy test reports sudden unilateral pelvic pain, dizziness" points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "A client with a positive pregnancy test reports sudden unilateral pelvic pain, dizziness" suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so "Light pink spotting only" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.f: "Improving pelvic discomfort after rest" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "A client with a positive pregnancy test reports sudden unilateral pelvic pain, dizziness" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: When pregnancy, unilateral pain, shoulder pain, and shock signs appear together, think ruptured ectopic fast.
questions.speedCue: Pregnancy plus unilateral pain plus shoulder pain plus hypotension is a hemorrhage pattern, not a routine discomfort pattern.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which findings in this febrile client support the nurse's concern for meningococcal sepsis? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A college student arrives with fever, severe headache, malaise, and rapidly worsening illness.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding which additional findings would strengthen concern for a meningococcal emergency rather than a routine viral illness.

options.text: A nonblanching petechial or purpuric rash
Hypotension or rapidly worsening perfusion
Neck stiffness and photophobia
A mild itchy rash that fades with pressure
Warm, stable vitals with improving symptoms after fluids
Acute confusion or worsening mental status

questions.rationale: Meningococcal sepsis clues cluster around invasive infection and rapid deterioration: nonblanching rash, perfusion decline, meningeal irritation, and mental-status change. The nurse should exclude benign or improving patterns that do not fit invasive sepsis.

distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "rapidly worsening illness" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.e: The cue "Meningococcal sepsis clues cluster around invasive infection and rapid deterioration: nonblanching rash, perfusion decline, meningeal irritation" suggests infection progression, so "Warm, stable vitals with improving symptoms after fluids" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: The fast clue is not fever alone, but fever plus rash and collapse signs.
questions.speedCue: Nonblanching rash plus neuro change or shock is the shortcut.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

structuredRationale.overview: The safest answer is A: A nonblanching petechial or purpuric rash; B: Hypotension or rapidly worsening perfusion; C: Neck stiffness and photophobia; F: Acute confusion or worsening mental status. Meningococcal sepsis clues cluster around invasive infection and rapid deterioration: nonblanching rash, perfusion decline, meningeal irritation, and mental-status change. The nurse should exclude benign or improving patterns that do not fit invasive sepsis. questions.stem: A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse sees a loop of umbilical cord. Which immediate actions should the nurse take?
questions.scenario: The fetal heart rate remains in the 70s while help is being called.

options.text: Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord
Position the client to relieve pressure on the cord
Attempt to push the visible cord back into the vagina
Delay intervention until the provider is at the bedside
Call for immediate obstetric assistance
Start coached pushing with the next contraction

questions.rationale: Cord prolapse is a compression emergency. The nurse's first moves are to relieve the pressure, position the client to reduce compression, and summon immediate obstetric help. Pushing the cord back or delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk.

distractorRationales.c: "Attempt to push the visible cord back into the vagina" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Cord prolapse is a compression emergency" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: "Delay intervention until the provider is at the bedside" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Cord prolapse is a compression emergency" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.f: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord plus Position the client" fits the safer response to the cue "A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse".

questions.takeaway: Cord prolapse means relieve compression now, not when the provider arrives.
questions.speedCue: If you can see the cord and the heart rate crashes, think pressure-off-the-cord immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which findings make severe symptomatic hyponatremia the best interpretation? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A client taking a thiazide diuretic becomes progressively more confused during admission.

options.text: New confusion with headache
Seizure activity after worsening mentation
Serum sodium of 118 mEq/L
Dry hacking cough for 1 week
Normal orientation and steady gait

Start coached pushing with the next contraction

questions.stem: Which findings make severe symptomatic hyponatremia the best interpretation? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A client taking a thiazide diuretic becomes progressively more confused during admission.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding whether the change is mostly electrolyte collapse or routine fatigue.

options.text: New confusion with headache
Seizure activity after worsening mentation
Serum sodium of 118 mEq/L
Dry hacking cough for 1 week
Normal orientation and steady gait

questions.rationale: The key cluster is a very low sodium level plus acute neurologic deterioration. The nurse should read the lab and the brain symptoms together, because that combination changes the urgency immediately.

distractorRationales.d: "Dry hacking cough for 1 week" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The key cluster is a very low sodium level plus acute neurologic deterioration" points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.e: "Normal orientation and steady gait" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "The nurse is deciding whether the change is mostly electrolyte collapse or routine fatigue" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before reassurance.

questions.takeaway: A sodium number matters more when the nervous system is already failing in front of you.
questions.speedCue: Low sodium plus neuro change is the shortcut; don't split the clue cluster apart.

questions.stem: Place the nursing actions in the order that best supports a central-line insertion bundle to reduce bloodstream infection risk.
questions.scenario: A provider is preparing to insert a central venous catheter at the bedside.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is responsible for bundle support and stopping the procedure if sterile breaks occur.

options.text: Perform hand hygiene and confirm the sterile setup is complete
Use maximal barrier precautions and proper skin antisepsis
Pause the procedure if sterile technique is broken
Document line necessity and bundle completion after insertion

questions.rationale: CLABSI prevention depends on hand hygiene, maximal barrier technique, chlorhexidine skin prep, speaking up about sterility breaks, and documenting bundle adherence. The nurse should not treat a sterile break as a minor issue when the line is still being placed.
questions.takeaway: Sterile discipline is part of the line procedure, not optional aftercare.
questions.speedCue: If the sterile field breaks, the next move is stop, not excuse.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for C. difficile. Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The nurse receives the result just before transferring the client to a semi-private room.
questions.additionalInfo: The unit has gowns, gloves, soap-and-water handwashing sinks, and sporicidal disinfectant available.

options.text: Initiate contact-plus-enteric precautions with gown and gloves
Place the client in an appropriate room and dedicate equipment when possible
Perform soap-and-water hand hygiene after care
Ensure the environment is cleaned with a sporicidal product

questions.rationale: C. difficile prevention works as a bundle: start precautions, protect room placement and equipment, use soap-and-water hand hygiene, and ensure sporicidal cleaning. The sequence keeps spores from spreading while also protecting the next patient environment.
questions.takeaway: For C. difficile, think gloves and gown, room protection, soap-and-water, then sporicidal cleaning.
questions.speedCue: The shortcut is spores: alcohol gel alone is not enough.

questions.stem: A client one hour after delivery has heavy bleeding and a boggy uterus. Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The pad is saturated and the client reports dizziness while holding the newborn.

options.text: Massage the fundus
Call for help and bring hemorrhage support to the bedside
Assess bladder fullness and assist with emptying if needed
Prepare uterotonic medication after initial bedside measures

questions.rationale: Heavy postpartum bleeding with a boggy uterus is managed as a hemorrhage problem first. The nurse brings help, massages the fundus, corrects bladder-related atony contributors, and prepares uterotonic support without waiting for routine reassessment.
questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus means act on atony now, not after more observation.
questions.speedCue: On exam day, boggy plus bleeding should push uterine atony to the top immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with sodium 118 mEq/L becomes confused and then has a seizure. Place the actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: Emergency supplies are available and the provider is on the way.

options.text: Prepare to administer hypertonic saline as prescribed
Protect the airway and initiate seizure precautions
Notify the provider or rapid response team with the acute change
Reassess neurologic status after initial stabilization

questions.rationale: In symptomatic hyponatremia, the nurse starts with seizure safety and airway protection, escalates immediately, prepares for controlled hypertonic saline correction, and then reassesses neurologic response. questions.stem: Place the nursing actions in the order that best follows fire safety priorities when smoke is seen near an oxygen source.
questions.scenario: Smoke is seen near an electrical device in a client's room while the client is receiving oxygen.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is awake but weak and needs assistance to leave the room safely.

options.text: Rescue the client from immediate danger
Activate the alarm and call for help
Contain the fire by closing doors and managing the room space
Extinguish only if the fire is small and safe to approach with the correct device

questions.rationale: RACE still governs the first moves: rescue, alarm, contain, then extinguish if safe. Oxygen and electrical fire clues raise urgency, so the nurse removes the client from danger before trying to manage equipment or flames.
questions.takeaway: Rescue comes before equipment cleanup when smoke and oxygen are in the same room.
questions.speedCue: Smoke plus oxygen means RACE immediately, not troubleshooting first.

visualRationale.title: fire_safety_RACE_PASS_oxygen
visualRationale.caption: Rescue comes before equipment cleanup when smoke and oxygen are in the same room.
visualRationale.conclusion: Rescue comes before equipment cleanup when smoke and oxygen are in the same room.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move when a newborn feed is painful and ineffective?
questions.scenario: A postpartum client says breastfeeding is sharply painful and the newborn falls asleep after a few shallow sucks.
questions.additionalInfo: The infant has had fewer wet diapers since discharge teaching began earlier that morning.

options.text: Assess latch and positioning, then support a deeper latch with feeding observation
Tell the parent to limit feeds because nipple pain means the baby is feeding too often
Switch to water between feeds until the nipples heal
Recommend stopping breastfeeding for the rest of the day without reassessment

questions.rationale: Pain with shallow ineffective sucking points to latch and transfer problems, not simply normal soreness. The nurse should reassess positioning and feeding effectiveness before recommending delays or supplements that do not fix the cause.

distractorRationales.b: "Tell the parent to limit feeds because nipple pain means the baby is feeding too often" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Pain with shallow ineffective sucking points to latch and transfer problems, not simply normal soreness" points to pediatric or newborn instability. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.
distractorRationales.c: "Switch to water between feeds until the nipples heal" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "Pain with shallow ineffective sucking points to latch and transfer problems, not simply normal soreness" raises concern for pediatric or newborn instability, so maternal-fetal stabilization is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.d: "Recommend stopping breastfeeding for the rest of the day without reassessment" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Pain with shallow ineffective sucking points to latch and transfer problems, not simply normal soreness" points to pediatric or newborn instability. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.

questions.takeaway: Pain plus poor output means reassess latch, not just reassure.
questions.speedCue: Painful shallow sucks and fewer wets point to latch failure first.

visualRationale.caption: Pain plus poor output means reassess latch, not just reassure.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move in this infarction-related hypotension?
questions.scenario: A client with an inferior STEMI becomes more hypotensive after receiving nitroglycerin.

options.text: Right ventricular infarction with preload dependence, making nitrates poorly tolerated
Expected medication relaxation without perfusion significance
Simple dehydration that only needs oral fluids
A benign anxiety episode triggered by chest pain

questions.rationale: Inferior MI plus hypotension after nitrates with clear lungs suggests a right-sided infarction that needs preload. The nurse should answer from hemodynamic dependence, not from generic antianginal expectations.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "rising jugular venous pressure" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Expected medication relaxation without perfusion significance" would not correct the active problem. questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move when this client becomes increasingly paranoid and agitated?
questions.scenario: A client with schizophrenia is pacing, shouting that staff members are trying to poison him, and clenching his fists.
questions.additionalInfo: The unit is noisy, and several other clients have gathered nearby to watch the scene.

options.text: This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal of excess triggers
The nurse should argue with the delusion to prove the belief is false
The nurse should bring more clients close by to normalize the interaction
The nurse should leave immediately without setting limits or assessing risk

questions.rationale: Acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation, and calm limit setting rather than confrontation. Arguing about the delusion or adding more external stimulation increases the risk that the client's distress escalates into violence.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation" suggests clinical safety risk, so "The nurse should argue with the delusion to prove the belief is false" would not correct the active problem. distractorRationales.c: The cue "Acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation" suggests clinical safety risk, so "The nurse should bring more clients close by to normalize the interaction" would not correct the active problem. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal" fits the safer response to the cue "In psychosis, the nurse leads with safety and calm structure, not debate".

questions.takeaway: In psychosis, the nurse leads with safety and calm structure, not debate.
questions.speedCue: Paranoia plus clenched fists means lower the room before you lower the delusion.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move when a client taking warfarin shows bleeding risk and a rising INR?
questions.scenario: A client taking warfarin for atrial fibrillation reports black stools and bleeding gums.
questions.additionalInfo: Today's INR is markedly above therapeutic range and the next dose is due soon.

options.text: Hold the dose, assess bleeding severity, and escalate reversal planning per protocol

questions.rationale: Bleeding symptoms with a supratherapeutic INR require immediate hold-and-escalate thinking, not routine dosing. The nurse should assess the extent of bleeding and follow provider/protocol guidance for reversal or dose adjustment.

distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "Bleeding symptoms with a supratherapeutic INR require immediate hold-and-escalate thinking, not routine dosing" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move when this client becomes increasingly paranoid and agitated?
questions.scenario: A client with schizophrenia is pacing, shouting that staff members are trying to poison him, and clenching his fists.
questions.additionalInfo: The unit is noisy, and several other clients have gathered nearby to watch the scene.

options.text: This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal of excess triggers

The nurse should leave immediately without setting limits or assessing risk

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move before and after a lumbar puncture?
questions.scenario: A client is scheduled for a lumbar puncture to evaluate suspected meningitis.
questions.additionalInfo: After the procedure, the client reports a severe headache that worsens when sitting upright.

options.text: Support correct positioning for the procedure and recognize post-dural headache as a complication to report and manage
Keep the client prone after the procedure and encourage repeated ambulation to clear the headache
Tell the client the headache proves the test was successful and needs no follow-up
Delay provider notification because headache is expected after any spinal procedure

questions.rationale: The nurse positions the client to widen spinal spaces and then monitors for complications such as post-dural headache. Worsening upright headache after lumbar puncture is not a shrug-off finding and should be reported and managed.

distractorRationales.b: "Keep the client prone after the procedure and encourage repeated ambulation to clear the headache" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "After the procedure, the client reports a severe headache that worsens when sitting upright" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.c: "Tell the client the headache proves the test was successful and needs no follow-up" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "After the procedure, the client reports a severe headache that worsens when sitting upright" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Delay provider notification because headache is expected after any spinal procedure" moves care toward routine observation before bedside safety action.

questions.takeaway: Post-lumbar-puncture headache is a complication clue, not just routine discomfort.
questions.speedCue: Headache worse upright after LP means think complication, not reassurance.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move in this infarction-related hypotension?
questions.scenario: A client with an inferior STEMI becomes more hypotensive after receiving nitroglycerin.
questions.additionalInfo: The client has clear lungs, cool skin, and rising jugular venous pressure.

options.text: Right ventricular infarction with preload dependence, making nitrates poorly tolerated
Expected medication relaxation without perfusion significance
Simple dehydration that only needs oral fluids
A benign anxiety episode triggered by chest pain

questions.rationale: Inferior MI plus hypotension after nitrates with clear lungs suggests a right-sided infarction that needs preload. The nurse should answer from hemodynamic dependence, not from generic antianginal expectations.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Inferior MI plus hypotension after nitrates with clear lungs suggests a right-sided infarction that needs preload" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Expected medication relaxation without perfusion significance" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Hypotension after nitrates with clear lungs is a clue that preload matters" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "A benign anxiety episode triggered by chest pain" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "rising jugular venous pressure" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Hypotension after nitrates with clear lungs is a clue that preload matters.
questions.speedCue: Inferior MI plus nitrate drop plus JVD is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move when family demands conflict with a documented client directive?
questions.scenario: A client with metastatic cancer has a signed advance directive declining intubation and CPR.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is now unresponsive, and two adult children insist the nurse should 'do everything' while the provider is en route.

options.text: Honor the documented advance directive while escalating the conflict through the provider and ethics chain
Ignore the directive because family requests automatically override prior written wishes
Delay all action until the family agrees on a plan
Ask the family to sign a new code-status form at the bedside before doing anything else

questions.rationale: A valid advance directive reflects the client's expressed wishes and should guide care when the client can no longer speak. The nurse should follow that documented plan while escalating the family conflict through the proper chain rather than treating family pressure as a replacement for the client's autonomy.

distractorRationales.b: "Ignore the directive because family requests automatically override prior written wishes" misses the bedside risk. The cue "advance directive conflict family safety" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Honor the documented advance directive while escalating the conflict through the provider and ethics chain" fits the safer response to the cue "advance directive conflict family safety".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Ask the family to sign a new code-status form at the bedside before doing anything else" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.

questions.takeaway: When family demands conflict with a valid directive, the client's documented wishes stay at the center of care.
questions.speedCue: The shortcut is autonomy first: a signed directive is the client's voice when the family is distressed.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority before this client receives IV contrast?
questions.scenario: A client with chronic kidney disease is scheduled for a contrast-enhanced CT study.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse notes elevated creatinine and asks which prevention frame should guide care before transport.

options.text: Identify renal vulnerability, protect hydration and kidney-safe preparation, and watch post-contrast renal trends
Treat the study as risk-free because contrast only affects clients on dialysis
Delay hydration because IV fluids can interfere with the scanner
Ignore baseline creatinine as long as the client is not reporting pain

questions.rationale: Contrast prevention starts with recognizing renal vulnerability and supporting kidney-protective preparation, especially hydration and medication review when ordered. The nurse should not treat contrast risk as absent just because the client is stable at the bedside.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Contrast prevention starts with recognizing renal vulnerability and supporting kidney-protective preparation, especially hydration and medication review when ordered" suggests medication toxicity, so "Treat the study as risk-free because contrast only affects clients on dialysis" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "Contrast prevention starts with recognizing renal vulnerability and supporting kidney-protective preparation, especially hydration and medication review when ordered" suggests medication toxicity, so "Delay hydration because IV fluids can interfere with the scanner" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Ignore baseline creatinine as long as the client is not reporting pain" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before medication-safety intervention.

questions.takeaway: Stable appearance does not erase contrast risk when the kidneys are already vulnerable.
questions.speedCue: High creatinine before contrast means prevention mode, not routine transport mode.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority after an adolescent screens positive for depression?
questions.scenario: A 16-year-old scores in the moderate range on a depression screening tool during a school-based clinic visit.
questions.additionalInfo: The adolescent shrugs and says, 'I'm just tired all the time. It's not a big deal.'

options.text: Treat the screen as meaningful, assess safety directly, and connect the adolescent with follow-up support
Reassure the adolescent that screening tools often overreact and no follow-up is needed
Skip all direct questions about self-harm because they may plant the idea
Wait until the next annual visit to revisit mood symptoms

questions.rationale: A positive depression screen is a prompt for direct safety assessment and follow-up support, not a reassurance cue. The nurse should not avoid suicide questions or delay referral simply because the adolescent is minimizing symptoms.

distractorRationales.b: "Reassure the adolescent that screening tools often overreact and no follow-up is needed" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Screening only helps when a positive result leads to direct safety assessment and follow-up" points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "A positive depression screen is a prompt for direct safety assessment and follow-up support, not a reassurance cue" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Wait until the next annual visit to revisit mood symptoms" moves care toward routine observation before bedside safety action.

questions.takeaway: Screening only helps when a positive result leads to direct safety assessment and follow-up.
questions.speedCue: A positive screen means ask directly, not later.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority after recognizing that the wrong dose of insulin was given?
questions.scenario: The nurse discovers that 10 units of rapid-acting insulin were administered instead of the prescribed 4 units.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is awake but now diaphoretic and shaky, and the blood glucose result is pending.

options.text: Treat the client as the priority, assess and intervene for hypoglycemia, notify the provider, then complete the reporting chain
Complete the incident report before assessing the client so the timeline is accurate
Wait for symptoms to worsen so the provider has clear evidence of harm
Ask the next nurse to decide whether the error needs to be disclosed

questions.rationale: After a medication error, the unstable client comes before paperwork. The nurse should assess and treat the potential hypoglycemia first, notify the provider, and then complete the required reporting process rather than allowing documentation to delay urgent care.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Treat the client as the priority, assess and intervene for hypoglycemia, notify the provider, then complete" fits the safer response to the cue "The nurse should assess and treat the potential hypoglycemia first, notify the provider".
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "Wait for symptoms to worsen so the provider has clear evidence of harm" moves care toward routine observation before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "The nurse discovers that 10 units of rapid-acting insulin were administered instead of the prescribed 4 units" suggests unstable glucose physiology, so "Ask the next nurse to decide whether the error needs to be disclosed" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Reporting matters, but the harmed or unstable client always comes first.
questions.speedCue: Error chain shortcut: patient first, provider second, paperwork third.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority for refractory dyspnea in a palliative-care client?
questions.scenario: A client with end-stage pulmonary disease reports air hunger despite oxygen and appears frightened.
questions.additionalInfo: The family asks whether increasing comfort medications means the team is giving up.

options.text: Treat dyspnea as a symptom crisis, use prescribed comfort measures, and coach the family on the goal of relief
Avoid symptom medication because sedation always means care is no longer active
Stop all oxygen and repositioning because they no longer matter in palliative care
Delay symptom relief until the family agrees on every part of the plan

questions.rationale: Palliative care actively treats dyspnea and distress, and comfort-focused medication can be appropriate when prescribed. The nurse should frame the goal as relief of suffering, not abandonment of care.

distractorRationales.b: "Avoid symptom medication because sedation always means care is no longer active" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Palliative care actively treats dyspnea and distress" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Stop all oxygen and repositioning because they no longer matter in palliative care" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Palliative care actively treats dyspnea and distress" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Comfort care still treats symptoms aggressively when the goal is relief.
questions.speedCue: Air hunger in palliative care is a treat-now symptom problem, not a wait-for-consensus problem.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which conclusion best fits this newborn's change in status?
questions.scenario: A 22-hour-old newborn is feeding poorly, becomes limp during assessment, and has episodes of shallow breathing with temperature 36.0 C (96.8 F).
questions.additionalInfo: The parent says the baby was more alert earlier and is now difficult to wake for feeds.

options.text: Early neonatal sepsis pattern requiring prompt escalation and full sepsis evaluation
Normal first-day newborn sleepiness with no urgent concern
Transient feeding intolerance only
Expected cooling that will resolve with a blanket alone

questions.rationale: Poor feeding, lethargy, temperature instability, and breathing change create a high-risk neonatal sepsis pattern. The nurse should connect the instability cluster quickly instead of explaining each sign away separately.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "Early neonatal sepsis pattern requiring prompt escalation and full sepsis evaluation" fits the safer response to the cue "has episodes of shallow breathing with temperature 36".
distractorRationales.c: "Transient feeding intolerance only" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "breathing change create a high-risk neonatal sepsis pattern" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "breathing change create a high-risk neonatal sepsis pattern" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "Expected cooling that will resolve with a blanket alone" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Several subtle instability clues together should outweigh one reassuring explanation in a newborn.
questions.speedCue: When feeding, temperature, arousal, and breathing all drift the wrong way together, think sepsis early.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority after an adolescent screens positive for depression?
questions.scenario: A 16-year-old scores in the moderate range on a depression screening tool during a school-based clinic visit.
questions.additionalInfo: The adolescent shrugs and says, 'I'm just tired all the time. It's not a big deal.'

options.text: Treat the screen as meaningful, assess safety directly, and connect the adolescent with follow-up support
Reassure the adolescent that screening tools often overreact and no follow-up is needed
Skip all direct questions about self-harm because they may plant the idea
Wait until the next annual visit to revisit mood symptoms

questions.rationale: A positive depression screen is a prompt for direct safety assessment and follow-up support, not a reassurance cue. The nurse should not avoid suicide questions or delay referral simply because the adolescent is minimizing symptoms.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "A 16-year-old scores in the moderate range on a depression screening tool during a school-based clinic visit" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "A positive depression screen is a prompt for direct safety assessment and follow-up support, not a reassurance cue" suggests clinical safety risk, so "Skip all direct questions about self-harm because they may plant the idea" would not correct the active problem. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: "Wait until the next annual visit to revisit mood symptoms" misses the bedside risk. The cue "A 16-year-old scores in the moderate range on a depression screening tool during a school-based clinic visit" points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Screening only helps when a positive result leads to direct safety assessment and follow-up.
questions.speedCue: A positive screen means ask directly, not later.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with C. difficile (CDI) colitis. Which instruction is MOST important to give the nursing assistant assigned to this patient's room?

options.text: Use alcohol-based hand sanitizer after all patient contact to prevent C. diff spread
Wear gloves and gown for all contact with the patient and environment, and wash hands with soap and water before leaving the room
Wear only gloves since C. diff is transmitted via droplets
Apply an N95 respirator before entering the room due to airborne risk

questions.rationale: C. difficile is transmitted via the fecal-oral route through hardy spores. Key isolation requirements: contact precautions (gloves AND gown for all room entry), and crucially, soap and water handwashing rather than alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR). C. diff spores are NOT killed by alcohol-based sanitizers — soap and water physically removes spores from hands. ABHR is not effective against C. diff. C. diff is not airborne or droplet-transmitted, so N95s are not required. Gloves alone (without gown) are insufficient for contact precautions.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of patient teaching and medication-safety reinforcement. The stem cue "diff is not airborne or droplet-transmitted, so N95s are not required" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Wear only gloves since C. diff is transmitted via droplets" moves care toward routine care before patient teaching and medication-safety reinforcement.
distractorRationales.d: "Apply an N95 respirator before entering the room due to airborne risk" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Key isolation requirements: contact precautions (gloves AND gown for all room entry)" points to infection progression. Teaching items should reinforce the specific medication, symptom, or safety rule rather than inventing a new emergency.

questions.takeaway: C. diff = contact precautions (gloves + gown) + soap and water handwashing. Alcohol hand sanitizer does NOT kill C. diff spores. This is one of the few situations where soap/water is explicitly superior to ABHR.

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.

Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.stem: A patient with pneumonia is breathing faster, using accessory muscles, and says, "I am getting tired." What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Reassess breathing and oxygenation immediately
Encourage the patient to save energy and wait
Turn off the oxygen so the patient can rest
Document that fatigue is expected with infection

questions.rationale: Fatigue with escalating work of breathing may signal respiratory failure. The nurse should reassess the patient right away. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Waiting can miss deterioration. - C Removing oxygen is unsafe without reassessment. - D Fatigue can be a warning sign, not just expected illness.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A patient with pneumonia is breathing faster, using accessory muscles" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "Turn off the oxygen so the patient can rest" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Fatigue with escalating work of breathing may signal respiratory failure" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: "Document that fatigue is expected with infection" misses the bedside risk. The cue "- C Removing oxygen is unsafe without reassessment" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: When a patient says they are tiring, treat that as a respiratory warning.
